questions.question: Section 10(1) NFOATP 1997 creates the offence of harassment. What are the essential elements of this offence?

options.A: That the accused communicated with the complainant on a single occasion without the complainant's consent, causing them distress
options.B: That the accused, without lawful authority or reasonable excuse, persistently, by their acts, intentionally or recklessly, seriously interferes with the other's peace and privacy or causes alarm, distress or harm to the other, in circumstances where a reasonable person would realise such acts would have this effect
options.C: That the accused followed the complainant on at least five separate occasions and photographed them without consent
options.D: That the accused made threatening communications to the complainant with intent to cause fear of immediate harm

questions.explanation: Section 10(1) NFOATP 1997 harassment requires: (1) No lawful authority or reasonable excuse; (2) Persistence — the conduct must be repeated, not merely a single incident; (3) The acts, intentionally or recklessly; (4) Seriously interfere with the other's peace and privacy, OR cause alarm, distress or harm to the other; (5) An objective test — a reasonable person would realise the acts would have this effect. The persistence requirement is the most critical distinguishing feature of harassment — it separates it from a one-off incident. The objective reasonable person test is the second key element. Option A is wrong because harassment requires persistence (multiple acts) and more than mere non-consented communication. Option C invents specific conduct requirements (following five times, photographing) that are not in the section. Option D describes s.5 threats, not s.10(1) harassment.

questions.question: Section 10(2) NFOATP 1997 creates a distinct stalking offence. How does stalking under s.10(2) differ from harassment under s.10(1)?

options.A: Stalking requires more incidents than harassment — you need at least 10 separate contacts before stalking is made out
options.B: The critical differences are: stalking under s.10(2) does NOT require persistence (a single act can suffice); stalking requires fear of violence against the complainant or another, OR serious alarm/distress with substantial adverse impact on daily activities; harassment s.10(1) requires persistence AND harm/alarm/distress or serious interference with peace and privacy
options.C: The difference is purely about the medium — s.10(1) covers in-person harassment; s.10(2) covers electronic stalking
options.D: There is no difference — s.10(1) and s.10(2) are identical offences prosecuted under different numbers

questions.explanation: The distinction between s.10(1) harassment and s.10(2) stalking is substantive and exam-critical: (1) Persistence: harassment requires persistent (repeated) acts; stalking can be established from a single act; (2) Effect required: harassment requires serious interference with peace and privacy OR alarm, distress or harm; stalking requires either (a) fear that violence will be used against the complainant or a connected person, OR (b) serious alarm or distress that has a substantial adverse impact on usual day-to-day activities; (3) Both share the mens rea (intentionally or recklessly) and the objective test (reasonable person standard). The same conduct can potentially give rise to both charges — a pattern of conduct causing fear of violence and disrupting daily life would satisfy both. Option A incorrectly quantifies the number of contacts required. Option C incorrectly limits the distinction to medium. Option D is wrong — the two offences have distinct elements.

questions.question: What new standalone offence was inserted into the NFOATP 1997 by the Criminal Justice (Miscellaneous Provisions) Act 2023?

options.B: Section 10A — a new standalone stalking offence, separate from and in addition to the existing s.10(2) stalking provision
options.D: Section 2A — aggravated assault with a weapon

questions.explanation: The Criminal Justice (Miscellaneous Provisions) Act 2023 (in force 1 November 2023) inserted two new provisions into the NFOATP 1997 relevant to this lesson: (1) Section 3A — strangulation and suffocation (covered in Lesson 5.5); (2) Section 10A — a new standalone stalking offence. Section 10A captures the same stalking conduct as s.10(2) but as a clearly labelled, identifiable standalone charge rather than a sub-section of the harassment provision. This makes the charging of stalking more transparent and provides a cleaner legislative framework for victims and courts. Section 10A also carries a maximum of 10 years imprisonment. The creation of s.10A reflects a broader European trend (following the Istanbul Convention) towards criminalising stalking as a specific, distinct offence. Option A is wrong about which provision was new (s.3A is also from CJ(MP)A 2023 but is a different lesson). Options C and D describe provisions that do not exist.

questions.question: Tom has been sending his ex-partner Laura unsolicited messages on five separate occasions over three weeks, monitoring her social media, driving past her home twice, and appearing outside her workplace on two occasions. Laura reports feeling frightened and has altered her daily routine to avoid potential contact with Tom. Which offence(s) should be considered?

options.A: S.10(1) harassment only — because the conduct is persistent
options.B: Both s.10(1) harassment and s.10(2)/s.10A stalking may apply — the persistence satisfies harassment; the conduct causes Laura serious alarm and distress with substantial adverse impact on her daily activities (she has altered her routine), satisfying the stalking element; the prosecution should charge both in the alternative
options.C: S.10(2) stalking only — harassment can only be charged if Tom has made physical contact
options.D: Neither — Tom must physically follow Laura on foot for harassment or stalking to apply; driving past her home is insufficient

questions.explanation: On these facts, both s.10(1) and s.10(2) are potentially applicable and should be charged in the alternative: (1) S.10(1) harassment — Tom's conduct is persistent (multiple messages, monitoring, driving past, appearing outside workplace over three weeks); the pattern seriously interferes with Laura's peace and privacy and causes distress; a reasonable person would recognise this effect; (2) S.10(2)/s.10A stalking — the conduct has caused Laura serious alarm or distress with substantial adverse impact on her daily activities (she has altered her routine to avoid Tom). Note that s.10(3) provides a non-exhaustive list of conduct capable of constituting s.10 offences, which includes: following, watching, monitoring, tracking, electronic communication interference — all present in Tom's conduct. Option A incorrectly limits the charge to harassment only. Option C incorrectly requires physical contact for harassment. Option D incorrectly restricts relevant conduct to on-foot following.

questions.question: The s.10(1) harassment offence requires that 'a reasonable person would realise' the conduct would have the effect of seriously interfering with peace and privacy or causing alarm, distress or harm. What is the significance of this objective test?

options.A: The test means the complainant's subjective distress is irrelevant — only what a reasonable person would experience matters
options.B: The objective test sets a floor — it prevents harassment charges from being brought for conduct that only an unusually sensitive complainant would find distressing; conduct must be such that the reasonable person would recognise its likely effect; the complainant's actual distress remains relevant but the objective standard prevents overly subjective findings
options.C: The objective test requires the accused to have consulted a reasonable person before engaging in the conduct
options.D: The objective test means the court must hear expert witness evidence on what a reasonable person would find distressing before returning a verdict

questions.explanation: The 'reasonable person' test in s.10(1) serves two functions: (1) It protects against convictions based purely on an unusually sensitive complainant's subjective distress — the accused is not responsible for harm that only an exceptionally sensitive person would experience from objectively innocuous conduct; (2) It protects complainants with unusual vulnerabilities — if a reasonable person would recognise that the conduct would cause the stated effects, the accused cannot escape liability by arguing the complainant over-reacted. The test is applied to the conduct itself and the circumstances. Critically, the complainant's actual distress remains relevant evidence — if the complainant is distressed, this supports (though does not conclusively establish) that a reasonable person would also be distressed. Option A goes too far — the complainant's distress is still relevant, it is simply not the sole determinant. Option C is absurd. Option D adds an expert witness requirement that has no statutory basis.

questions.question: A single incident of extremely threatening conduct that causes the complainant to alter their daily routine out of fear — such as a stranger following a person home and remaining outside their house for an hour, staring at them — may ground which offence without requiring proof of persistence?

options.A: Section 10(1) harassment — because a single serious incident satisfies the persistence requirement in exceptional cases
options.B: Section 10(2) stalking — because stalking does not require persistence; a single act causing fear of violence or serious alarm/distress with substantial adverse impact on daily activities can constitute stalking
options.C: Neither — without a pattern of conduct, no s.10 offence can be established
options.D: Section 2 assault — because the accused's presence outside the house amounts to a threatened assault

questions.explanation: Section 10(2) stalking explicitly does not require persistence — a single act of conduct can constitute stalking if it causes the complainant to fear violence or causes serious alarm or distress with a substantial adverse impact on their daily activities. A stranger following a person home and remaining outside their house for an hour, staring at them, is a single incident that: (a) would cause serious alarm or distress; and (b) would likely have a substantial adverse impact on the complainant's daily activities (they may be afraid to leave, return home, or use that route). The objective test is also satisfied — a reasonable person would recognise this effect. This example illustrates the key reason s.10(2) was drafted without a persistence requirement — sometimes a single, highly threatening incident is sufficient to constitute stalking behaviour. Option A is wrong — s.10(1) harassment requires persistence. Option C incorrectly requires multiple incidents for all s.10 offences. Option D is possible (s.2 apprehension-based assault) but s.10(2) is a more targeted and complete analysis for this scenario.

questions.question: What is the maximum sentence for harassment (s.10(1)) and stalking (s.10(2)) under the NFOATP 1997?

options.A: 6 months on summary conviction only — both are summary offences
options.B: 5 years on indictment — the original 1997 Act provision
options.C: 10 years on indictment — both offences carry this maximum
options.D: Life imprisonment — because stalking is treated as equivalent to serious harm

questions.explanation: Both harassment under s.10(1) and stalking under s.10(2)/s.10A NFOATP 1997 carry a maximum sentence of 10 years imprisonment on conviction on indictment. This maximum was increased from the original 1997 Act provision by the Criminal Justice (Miscellaneous Provisions) Act 2023, reflecting the growing recognition of the serious harm caused by persistent harassment and stalking. The offences are indictable and triable in the Circuit Court. Both offences are also triable as hybrid offences — summary conviction is possible with a lower penalty. Option A incorrectly describes these as summary-only offences. Option B states the pre-2023 maximum. Option D applies the s.4 causing serious harm/s.3A strangulation maximum.

questions.question: Section 10(3) NFOATP 1997 provides a list of types of conduct capable of constituting harassment or stalking. Is this list exhaustive?

options.A: Yes — only conduct falling within the listed categories can ground a s.10 charge
options.B: No — the s.10(3) list is non-exhaustive; it provides examples of conduct capable of constituting harassment or stalking but does not limit the offences to those specific types of conduct; courts can consider any conduct that satisfies the elements of s.10(1) or s.10(2)
options.C: Yes — the list is exhaustive and was comprehensively updated by the CJ(MP)A 2023 to include electronic stalking
options.D: The list is exhaustive for s.10(1) harassment but non-exhaustive for s.10(2) stalking

questions.explanation: Section 10(3) NFOATP 1997 provides a non-exhaustive illustrative list of the types of acts capable of constituting harassment or stalking. The list includes: following, watching, monitoring, tracking, spying, pestering, communicating with or about, impersonating, making threats or causing harm to, disclosing private information about, intercepting communications, loitering, interfering with property, taking photographs or other images, interfering with or monitoring electronic communications, and breaching court orders. This list is illustrative ('without prejudice to the generality of the foregoing') — it confirms that these specified acts can constitute harassment or stalking, but does not limit the offences to these acts. Courts can consider any conduct that satisfies the statutory elements. This flexibility is important as new forms of stalking behaviour (e.g., through emerging technologies) may not have been specifically listed but can still be charged. Options A and C incorrectly treat the list as exhaustive. Option D creates a false distinction between s.10(1) and s.10(2).

questions.question: What does 'connected person' mean in the context of s.10(2) stalking — the provision that includes fear of violence 'against that other or a connected person'?

options.A: 'Connected person' is not defined in the NFOATP 1997 — the term is ambiguous and has been criticised by courts
options.B: In the context of s.10, a connected person would typically be understood as a person closely associated with the complainant — a family member, partner, close friend, or other person whose safety is relevant to the complainant's fear; the fear of violence against this connected person motivates the complainant's fear and disrupts their daily activities
options.C: 'Connected person' means only blood relatives of the complainant
options.D: 'Connected person' refers only to other potential witnesses in criminal proceedings involving the accused

questions.explanation: The s.10(2) stalking provision encompasses fear of violence 'against that other or a connected person.' While the NFOATP 1997 does not provide a precise statutory definition of 'connected person' in s.10, the term is contextually understood to include persons closely associated with the complainant — family members, partners, children, and others whose safety the complainant has reasonable cause to fear for as a result of the stalker's conduct. This extension is important: a stalker may threaten or target not the primary complainant but their family members or associates, causing the complainant to live in fear for their loved ones. Such conduct satisfies the s.10(2) stalking element if it causes the complainant serious alarm or distress with substantial adverse daily impact, or fear that violence will be used against those connected persons. Option A incorrectly characterises the term as entirely undefined and criticised. Option C unduly restricts the term to blood relatives. Option D incorrectly limits the term to witnesses.
